module: When to start and Vascular access

sections.2.1 Absolute indications to start RRT:
Refractory hyperkalaemia: Refractory severe hyperkalemia despite medical treatment (bicarbonate, glucose-insulin infusion, beta2-agonists) is life-threatening and requires RRT. A specific threshold cannot be defined as severity depends on dynamics of serum potassium changes, metabolic acidosis, and CKD status. RRT is rarely urgent when serum potassium is below 6.0 mmol/L and almost always manageable with medical treatment.

Severe pulmonary oedema due to diuretic-resistant fluid overload: Fluid overload is frequent in severe AKI. With preserved diuresis, it can be managed by diuretics alone. The sole indication for RRT (with ultrafiltration goal) is severe deterioration of respiratory function and anuria despite diuretic therapy.

Poisoning by a dialysable agent: RRT is urgently required in several severe poisonings (e.g. metformin, lithium, ethylene glycol).
sections.2.2 Potential indications to start RRT:
Metabolic acidosis: Frequently observed in critically ill patients, worsened by AKI (increased plasma organic acids and unmeasured anions). Simple interventions include bicarbonate infusion (BICAR-ICU trial, Lancet 2018) or increased alveolar ventilation. Intractable metabolic acidosis with pH below 7.15 is a frequently quoted indication for RRT. RRT regulates acid-base by net addition of bicarbonate via dialysate/replacement fluid and removal of metabolic acids.

Fluid overload: Should only prompt urgent RRT when causing pulmonary edema with hypoxemia. Fluid accumulation prior to RRT initiation is associated with increased risk of death (FINNAKI study, Vaara et al. 2012), but association does not imply causation. Patients responsive to diuretics should not unnecessarily be exposed to RRT risks.

Uraemia: Complications (uremic pericarditis, pleural effusion, encephalopathy, coagulopathy) are unusual in AKI context. AKIKI-2 trial: urea threshold of 40 vs 50 mmol/L not associated with significant mortality difference at 60 days. However, in secondary analysis, the very delayed strategy was independently associated with worse outcome. When urea exceeds 40-50 mmol/L without renal function recovery, RRT should be considered.
sections.2.3 Conditions not requiring RRT:
Septic shock: The hypothesis that RRT may remove circulating toxins and inflammatory cytokines during sepsis has not been confirmed. Sepsis per se does not constitute an indication to start RRT. High volume and very high volume haemofiltration failed to improve haemodynamic parameters and survival.

Biomarkers: A meta-analysis (Klein et al. 2018) showed that plasma and urine biomarkers perform poorly for predicting the need for RRT and did not perform better than serum creatinine.

Duration of oligo-anuria: The duration of decreased urine output is not per se an indication to initiate RRT within the first 48h following severe AKI. The consequences of oligo-anuria (hyperkalemia, acidosis, fluid overload) are much more important for the decision.
sections.2.4 When to start RRT:
RRT is one of the most invasive ICU techniques. Known risks: catheter-related bloodstream infection, haemodynamic instability (severe/repeated hypotension), hypophosphatemia. Associated with increased workload and costs.

ELAIN trial: Single-center, 231 postsurgical patients. Reported advantage of early strategy, but ~75% had fluid overload/worsening pulmonary edema at baseline, potentially explaining the result.

AKIKI trial: Multi-center, 619 mixed ICU patients, KDIGO stage 3 AKI. No mortality difference (48% early vs 50% delayed, p=0.79). 49% in early arm never required RRT. Earlier renal recovery in delayed group. More RRT-free days in delayed group (17 vs 19, p<0.001).

IDEAL-ICU trial: Multi-center, 488 patients with septic shock, RIFLE F. No mortality difference (58% early vs 54% delayed, p=0.38). 38% in early arm never required RRT. More RRT-free days in delayed group (12 vs 16, p=0.006).

STARRT-AKI trial: 2927 ICU patients, stage 2-3 AKI without urgent indications. Accelerated strategy: RRT within 12h. Delayed strategy triggers: pH <7.20, bicarb <12, K+ >6, acute hypoxemic respiratory failure from fluid overload, or persistent AKI at 72h. No mortality difference at day 90 (43.9% vs 43.7%). 38.2% recovered without RRT in delayed group. Less RRT dependence at day 90 in delayed group (6% vs 10%, p<0.05).

AKIKI-2 trial: 278 patients, stage 3 AKI. Delayed vs very-delayed. No difference in RRT-free days at day 28. Day-60 mortality: 44% vs 55% (p=0.071). Only 21% in very-delayed group avoided RRT. Multivariate analysis: very-delayed strategy independently associated with increased mortality.

Conclusion: Clinicians now tend to prefer a delayed strategy, but excessive postponement may be deleterious.
sections.3.1 Vascular access location:
Approximately 10% of critically ill patients receive RRT during ICU stay. A well-functioning vascular access is mandatory for optimal RRT dose. Standard access: double lumen dialysis catheter in a large central vein.

Hagen-Poiseuille Law: Blood flow is a direct function of catheter diameter (wide diameter 12-16 Fr preferable) and inversely related to length. Best flows with catheter tip in high blood flow area (junction of right atrium and superior vena cava).

Superior vena cava territory: Best site is right internal jugular vein — short catheter (less resistance), no curves (laminar flow, no turbulences). Turbulences favor thromboses. Left internal jugular = more catheter dysfunctions. Subclavian should be avoided — may induce venous stenosis and compromise future AV fistula. Upper extremity access facilitates mobilisation. For BMI <24 kg/m2, jugular catheter bandage may be compromised by bone contours — femoral may be more appropriate for cachectic patients.

Inferior vena cava territory: Femoral catheter must be ≥25 cm to reach inferior vena cava. For BMI >28 kg/m2, femoral access = higher risk of catheter-related bloodstream infection — jugular more appropriate for obese patients.

KDIGO recommended order and catheter lengths:
1. Right internal jugular vein → 15 cm
2. Right or left femoral vein → 25 cm
3. Left internal jugular vein → 20 cm
4. Subclavian vein (dominant side preferred) → right 15 cm, left 20 cm
sections.3.2 Catheter properties and insertion:
Most suitable catheter: uncoated polyurethane or silicon, linear, cycle C-shape, without side holes, shotgun design with 2-3 cm distance between inflow and outflow tips to limit recirculation.

Recirculation: Occurs when newly dialyzed blood flows again into the same RRT circuit, diminishing effective dialysis dose. Three recommendations to limit recirculation:
1. Catheter distal tip in a high blood flow area
2. 2-3 cm between aspiration and reinjection holes
3. Avoid lumen reversal

Common mistake: Using too short (<25 cm) catheters in inferior vena cava territory — tip doesn't reach IVC, low surrounding blood flow, favoring recirculation.

Insertion: Ultrasound-guided to reduce haematoma, arterial puncture, pneumothorax. For superior vena cava territory: chest radiography mandatory before RRT initiation to check catheter tip position.

Guidewire exchange: Alternative to new venipuncture. Does not lead to more colonisations/infections but is associated with more catheter dysfunctions (Coupez et al. 2016).

Triple lumen catheters: Third lumen for drugs/fluids in emergencies or post-dialysis calcium infusion during regional citrate anticoagulation.
sections.3.3 Catheter locks:
Locking solutions maintain catheter patency and prevent infections. Citrate is interesting but only low-concentration citrate (4%) appears safe.

VERROUREA trial (Quenot et al. 2019): Randomized controlled trial comparing 4% trisodium citrate vs heparin locking solution. No benefit of citrate over heparin. Rates of catheter thromboses, catheter-related infections, and adverse events were not different between groups.
sections.3.4 Ten commandments:
The 'ten commandments' of the ideal RRT catheter (Huriaux et al. 2017):
1. Optimal external diameter 12-16 Fr, according to RRT modality
2. Optimal lumen shape is a cycle-C catheter
3. Optimal distal tip is shotgun tip catheter
4. Preferred insertion site is right internal jugular or any femoral vein
5. Avoid left internal jugular and subclavian insertion sites
6. Do not use line reversal
7. Use ultrasound guidance
8. Use lines made from biocompatible material
9. Check correct position with chest radiography (superior vena cava territory)
10. Remove catheter as soon as possible!

questions.question: Which of the following is an ABSOLUTE indication for initiating RRT in the ICU?
options: A: Serum potassium of 5.8 mmol/L responsive to insulin-glucose infusion
B: Metabolic acidosis with pH 7.18
C: Severe pulmonary oedema with anuria despite diuretic therapy
D: Blood urea nitrogen of 35 mmol/L with preserved diuresis
E: Oliguria lasting 36 hours
questions.explanation: The absolute indications for RRT are: refractory hyperkalemia despite medical treatment, severe pulmonary oedema due to diuretic-resistant fluid overload with anuria, and poisoning by a dialysable agent. A K+ of 5.8 responding to treatment, metabolic acidosis with pH 7.18 (potential but not absolute indication), elevated urea with preserved diuresis, and oliguria duration alone are not absolute indications.

questions.question: Below which serum potassium level is RRT rarely considered urgent?
options: A: 5.0 mmol/L
B: 5.5 mmol/L
C: 6.0 mmol/L
D: 6.5 mmol/L
E: 7.0 mmol/L
questions.explanation: RRT is rarely urgent when serum potassium is below 6.0 mmol/L, as hyperkalemia at this level is almost always manageable with medical treatment (bicarbonate, glucose-insulin, beta2-agonists). However, severity also depends on dynamics of change, metabolic acidosis, and CKD status.

questions.question: Regarding absolute indications for RRT initiation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Refractory hyperkalemia despite medical treatment is an absolute indication for RRT

statements.label: B
statements.text: Fluid overload with preserved diuresis responding to diuretics requires RRT

statements.label: C
statements.text: Lithium poisoning is an example of poisoning requiring urgent RRT

statements.label: D
statements.text: A specific potassium threshold can be defined for mandatory RRT initiation

questions.explanation: Refractory hyperkalemia is an absolute indication. Fluid overload responding to diuretics does NOT require RRT — the indication is anuria + respiratory deterioration despite diuretics. Lithium is a dialysable toxin requiring urgent RRT. No specific K+ threshold can be defined because severity depends on multiple factors (dynamics, acidosis, CKD).

questions.question: Below which pH is metabolic acidosis frequently quoted as a potential indication for RRT?
questions.explanation: Intractable metabolic acidosis with a pH below 7.15 is a frequently quoted potential indication for RRT. Before reaching this threshold, other interventions should be tried: bicarbonate infusion (BICAR-ICU trial) or increasing alveolar ventilation.

questions.question: According to the AKIKI-2 trial and current evidence, at what blood urea level should RRT be considered in the absence of renal function recovery?
options: A: 10-20 mmol/L
B: 20-30 mmol/L
C: 30-40 mmol/L
D: 40-50 mmol/L
E: >60 mmol/L
questions.explanation: When blood urea exceeds 40-50 mmol/L and there is no renal function recovery (usually observed as resumption of diuresis), RRT should be considered. The AKIKI-2 trial compared thresholds of 40 vs 50 mmol/L; while no significant mortality difference was found, secondary analysis showed the very-delayed strategy was independently associated with worse outcome.

questions.question: Regarding potential indications for RRT initiation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: RRT can correct metabolic acidosis by adding bicarbonate via dialysate and removing metabolic acids

statements.label: B
statements.text: Fluid accumulation prior to RRT initiation is associated with increased mortality risk

statements.label: C
statements.text: Uremic pericarditis is a common indication for RRT in acute kidney injury

statements.label: D
statements.text: Association between fluid overload and mortality implies that early RRT for fluid management is always beneficial

questions.explanation: RRT corrects acidosis in two ways: bicarbonate addition and acid removal. FINNAKI data showed fluid accumulation is associated with death risk. However, uremic complications (pericarditis, encephalopathy) are UNUSUAL in AKI. Association does not imply causation — patients responsive to diuretics should not be unnecessarily exposed to RRT risks.

questions.question: Which statement about RRT in septic shock is correct?
options: A: High-volume haemofiltration has been shown to improve survival in septic shock
B: Sepsis per se constitutes an indication to start RRT
C: RRT should be started early in all patients with septic shock to remove cytokines
D: High-volume and very high-volume haemofiltration have failed to improve haemodynamic parameters and survival
E: Plasma biomarkers reliably predict the need for RRT in sepsis
questions.explanation: The hypothesis that RRT may remove toxins and cytokines in sepsis has not been confirmed. High-volume and very high-volume haemofiltration failed to improve haemodynamics and survival. Sepsis per se is NOT an indication for RRT. Biomarkers perform poorly for RRT prediction (not better than creatinine).

questions.question: Regarding conditions that do NOT on their own require RRT:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Plasma and urine biomarkers perform better than serum creatinine for predicting RRT need

statements.label: B
statements.text: Duration of oliguria within the first 48 hours is per se an indication for RRT initiation

statements.label: C
statements.text: Septic shock alone does not constitute an indication to start RRT

statements.label: D
statements.text: The consequences of oligo-anuria (e.g. hyperkalemia, acidosis) are more important than its duration for deciding to start RRT

questions.explanation: Biomarkers perform POORLY and not better than creatinine (Klein 2018). Oliguria duration is NOT per se an indication. Sepsis per se is NOT an indication. The consequences of oligo-anuria (metabolic derangements) matter more than its duration.

questions.question: The AKIKI trial compared early vs delayed RRT initiation in 619 critically ill patients with KDIGO stage 3 AKI. Which of the following findings is correct?
options: A: Early strategy showed a significant survival benefit
B: Mortality was 48% in the early group and 50% in the delayed group with no significant difference
C: All patients in the early strategy group required RRT
D: Renal recovery was faster in the early strategy group
E: RRT-free days were similar between groups
questions.explanation: AKIKI showed no mortality difference (48% early vs 50% delayed, p=0.79). Importantly, 49% in the early arm never required RRT. Renal recovery occurred EARLIER in the delayed group, and the delayed group had more RRT-free days (17 vs 19, p<0.001).

questions.question: The STARRT-AKI trial enrolled 2927 patients with stage 2-3 AKI. Which statement about its results is correct?
options: A: Day-90 mortality was significantly lower in the accelerated strategy group
B: RRT dependence at day 90 was less probable in the delayed strategy group
C: The accelerated strategy reduced catheter-related infections
D: All patients in the delayed group eventually required RRT
E: The trial was stopped early due to clear superiority of the accelerated strategy
questions.explanation: STARRT-AKI showed no mortality difference (43.9% vs 43.7%). RRT dependence at day 90 was LESS probable in the delayed group (6% vs 10%, p<0.05). 38.2% in the delayed group recovered without RRT. The trial was not stopped early and showed no superiority for either strategy.

questions.question: Which trial was single-center, included mainly post-surgical patients, and reported an advantage of an early RRT strategy — but was potentially confounded by a high rate of baseline fluid overload?
options: A: AKIKI
B: IDEAL-ICU
C: STARRT-AKI
D: ELAIN
E: AKIKI-2
questions.explanation: The ELAIN trial was single-center, included 231 postsurgical (mainly cardiac surgery) patients, and reported an early strategy advantage. However, ~75% of patients had fluid overload or worsening pulmonary edema at baseline, potentially explaining the positive result. The larger multi-center trials (AKIKI, IDEAL-ICU, STARRT-AKI) did not confirm this finding.

questions.question: Regarding the timing of RRT initiation in critically ill patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The AKIKI and IDEAL-ICU trials both showed no mortality difference between early and delayed RRT strategies

statements.label: B
statements.text: In the AKIKI trial, approximately half of patients in the early arm never required RRT

statements.label: C
statements.text: The ELAIN trial was a large multi-center trial supporting delayed RRT initiation

statements.label: D
statements.text: The AKIKI-2 trial suggested that excessive postponement of RRT may be deleterious

questions.explanation: AKIKI and IDEAL-ICU both showed no mortality difference. In AKIKI, 49% in the early arm never needed RRT. ELAIN was a SINGLE-center trial of 231 patients that supported EARLY (not delayed) strategy. AKIKI-2 showed that the very-delayed strategy was independently associated with worse outcome in multivariate analysis.

questions.question: Regarding risks and complications of RRT that influence timing decisions:

statements.label: A
statements.text: RRT is associated with catheter-related bloodstream infections

statements.label: B
statements.text: Hypophosphatemia is a recognized complication of RRT

statements.label: C
statements.text: Delayed RRT initiation may allow spontaneous renal function recovery, potentially avoiding RRT altogether

statements.label: D
statements.text: Observational studies on RRT timing are reliable because they account for patients managed conservatively without RRT

questions.explanation: RRT risks include CRBSI, haemodynamic instability, and hypophosphatemia. Delayed initiation gives time for spontaneous recovery, which is why many patients randomized to early RRT never actually needed it. Observational studies were FLAWED because they did NOT account for patients who never received RRT despite severe AKI.

questions.question: According to KDIGO guidelines, what is the first-choice vascular access site for RRT in critically ill patients, and what catheter length should be used?
options: A: Left internal jugular vein, 20 cm
B: Right subclavian vein, 15 cm
C: Right internal jugular vein, 15 cm
D: Right femoral vein, 25 cm
E: Left femoral vein, 20 cm
questions.explanation: KDIGO recommends: 1st choice right internal jugular vein (15 cm), 2nd femoral vein (25 cm), 3rd left internal jugular vein (20 cm), 4th subclavian vein (right 15 cm, left 20 cm). The right IJ allows a short, straight catheter with laminar flow to the SVC-RA junction.

questions.question: Why should subclavian vein access be avoided for RRT dialysis catheters?
options: A: It is associated with higher rates of catheter-related bloodstream infection
B: It may induce venous stenosis and compromise a future arteriovenous fistula
C: The subclavian vein has insufficient diameter for a dialysis catheter
D: Ultrasound guidance is impossible for subclavian insertion
E: The subclavian route always causes pneumothorax
questions.explanation: Subclavian access may induce venous stenosis when the catheter is removed, which can compromise a potential future arteriovenous fistula for chronic dialysis. This is the main reason it is listed as last choice in the KDIGO guidelines.

questions.question: A cachectic patient (BMI 21 kg/m²) requires RRT. Which vascular access consideration is most relevant?
options: A: Subclavian access is preferred in cachectic patients
B: Femoral access may be more appropriate because jugular catheter bandage can be compromised by bone contours
C: Left internal jugular is preferred over right in thin patients
D: Shorter femoral catheters (15 cm) should be used due to low body weight
E: Cachectic patients do not need RRT
questions.explanation: For patients with BMI <24 kg/m², the bandage sealing for a jugular catheter may be compromised by bone contours. Therefore, femoral access might be more appropriate for cachectic patients. Note that femoral catheters must still be 25 cm to reach the IVC regardless of body habitus.

questions.question: Regarding vascular access location for RRT in critically ill patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: According to the Hagen-Poiseuille Law, blood flow is inversely related to catheter length

statements.label: B
statements.text: Left internal jugular vein access is associated with fewer catheter dysfunctions than the right

statements.label: C
statements.text: For BMI >28 kg/m², femoral access has a higher risk of catheter-related bloodstream infection

statements.label: D
statements.text: Femoral dialysis catheters should be at least 25 cm long to reach the inferior vena cava

questions.explanation: Hagen-Poiseuille: flow is directly related to diameter and inversely to length. LEFT IJ is associated with MORE (not fewer) catheter dysfunctions. For BMI >28, femoral access = higher CRBSI risk — jugular is more appropriate for obese patients. Femoral catheters must be ≥25 cm to reach the IVC.

questions.question: Regarding the KDIGO-recommended catheter lengths for RRT access:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Right internal jugular vein catheter: 15 cm

statements.label: B
statements.text: Femoral vein catheter: 15 cm

statements.label: C
statements.text: Left internal jugular vein catheter: 20 cm

statements.label: D
statements.text: Left subclavian vein catheter: 20 cm

questions.explanation: KDIGO recommended lengths: Right IJ = 15 cm, femoral = 25 cm (NOT 15 cm — must reach IVC), left IJ = 20 cm, subclavian = right 15 cm / left 20 cm. Too short femoral catheters (<25 cm) are a common mistake leading to poor flow and recirculation.

questions.question: Which of the following measures does NOT help reduce recirculation in a dialysis catheter?
options: A: Placing the catheter tip in a high blood flow area
B: Maintaining 2-3 cm between inflow and outflow tips
C: Reversing the catheter lumens when blood flow is inadequate
D: Using a catheter long enough to reach the IVC when using femoral access
E: Using a shotgun-design catheter tip
questions.explanation: Lumen reversal should be AVOIDED as it promotes recirculation — newly dialyzed blood re-enters the circuit. The three recommendations to limit recirculation are: tip in high flow area, 2-3 cm between aspiration/reinjection holes, and avoiding lumen reversal. Shotgun design with adequate tip separation also reduces recirculation.

questions.question: What is the purpose of performing a chest radiograph after inserting a dialysis catheter in the superior vena cava territory?
options: A: To confirm absence of pneumothorax only
B: To check the correct position of the catheter tip before initiating RRT
C: To measure the catheter diameter
D: To assess cardiac function before RRT
E: Chest radiography is not required for jugular catheters
questions.explanation: For catheters inserted in the superior vena cava territory, chest radiography is MANDATORY to check the correct position of the catheter tip before initiating the RRT session. This is one of the 'ten commandments' of the ideal RRT catheter.

questions.question: Regarding dialysis catheter properties and insertion technique:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The ideal catheter has a shotgun design with 2-3 cm between inflow and outflow tips

statements.label: B
statements.text: Ultrasound-guided insertion reduces the incidence of haematoma, arterial puncture and pneumothorax

statements.label: C
statements.text: Guidewire exchange leads to more catheter colonisations compared to new venipuncture

statements.label: D
statements.text: Side holes in the catheter are recommended to improve blood flow

questions.explanation: Shotgun design with 2-3 cm tip distance limits recirculation. US-guided insertion reduces complications. Guidewire exchange does NOT cause more colonisations/infections but IS associated with more catheter DYSFUNCTIONS (Coupez 2016). The ideal catheter is WITHOUT side holes.

questions.question: Regarding recirculation in dialysis catheters:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Recirculation diminishes the effective overall dialysis dose

statements.label: B
statements.text: Using too short femoral catheters (<25 cm) favors recirculation

statements.label: C
statements.text: Lumen reversal is recommended when blood flow is insufficient

statements.label: D
statements.text: A triple lumen catheter's third lumen can be used for post-dialysis calcium infusion during citrate anticoagulation

questions.explanation: Recirculation reduces effective dialysis dose. Short femoral catheters don't reach the IVC, creating low surrounding flow and recirculation. Lumen reversal should be AVOIDED (promotes recirculation). Triple lumen catheters have a third lumen usable for calcium infusion in citrate anticoagulation or for emergency drug administration.

questions.question: The VERROUREA trial compared catheter lock solutions for non-tunneled haemodialysis catheters. What was the main finding?
options: A: 4% trisodium citrate was superior to heparin for preventing catheter thrombosis
B: Heparin was superior to citrate for preventing catheter-related infections
C: No difference between 4% trisodium citrate and heparin in catheter thromboses, infections, or adverse events
D: High-concentration citrate (30%) was safer than 4% citrate
E: Both lock solutions should be avoided in critically ill patients
questions.explanation: The VERROUREA RCT found no benefit of 4% trisodium citrate compared to heparin locking solution. Rates of catheter thromboses, catheter-related infections, and adverse events were not different between groups. Only low-concentration citrate (4%) appears safe.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 'ten commandments' of the ideal RRT catheter?
options: A: Use ultrasound guidance for insertion
B: Check position with chest radiography for superior vena cava territory
C: Optimal external diameter is 12-16 Fr
D: Preferred insertion site is the subclavian vein
E: Remove catheter as soon as possible
questions.explanation: The preferred insertion sites according to the ten commandments are the right internal jugular or any femoral vein. The subclavian site should be AVOIDED (commandment 5) because of the risk of venous stenosis compromising future AV fistula access.

questions.question: Regarding the 'ten commandments' of the ideal RRT catheter:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Optimal lumen shape is a cycle-C catheter

statements.label: B
statements.text: Line reversal is acceptable when blood flow is suboptimal

statements.label: C
statements.text: Lines should be made from biocompatible material

statements.label: D
statements.text: The catheter should be kept in place as long as possible to avoid reinsertion

questions.explanation: Commandment 2: cycle-C catheter shape is optimal. Commandment 6: do NOT use line reversal. Commandment 8: use biocompatible material. Commandment 10: REMOVE catheter as soon as possible — keeping it longer than necessary increases infection risk.
